### Post by hoppipolla on 2009-10-28
Sup people's :)

Basically, usually the solution would lie in the KDE/Gnome settings or in Xorg.conf, but here is my xorg.conf:

```
# xorg.conf (X.Org X Window System server configuration file)
#                                                            
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using
# values from the debconf database.                                         
#                                                                           
# Edit this file with caution, and see the xorg.conf manual page.           
# (Type "man xorg.conf" at the shell prompt.)                               
#                                                                           
# This file is automatically updated on xserver-xorg package upgrades *only*
# if it has not been modified since the last upgrade of the xserver-xorg    
# package.                                                                  
#                                                                           
# Note that some configuration settings that could be done previously       
# in this file, now are automatically configured by the server and settings 
# here are ignored.                                                         
#                                                                           
# If you have edited this file but would like it to be automatically updated
# again, run the following command:                                         
#   sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg                               

Section "Monitor"
        Identifier      "Configured Monitor"
EndSection                                  

Section "Screen"
        Identifier      "Default Screen"
        Monitor         "Configured Monitor"
        Device          "Configured Video Device"
        DefaultDepth    24                       
EndSection                                       

Section "Module"
        Load    "glx"
EndSection

Section "Device"
        Identifier      "Configured Video Device"
        Driver  "nvidia"
        Option  "NoLogo"        "True"
EndSection
```

Do you see the problem? I don't know where these things are actually configured! lol

I did try the nvidia drivers settings but I couldn't find much there.

The reason I want to do this is every time I try to run a game, it goes out of my monitor's range.

What do you think?


Hoppi :)

---

### Post by Wim Sturkenboom on 2009-10-28
```

Section "Monitor"
    Identifier     "Generic Monitor"
[B]    HorizSync       27.0 - 96.0
    VertRefresh     50.0 - 160.0[/B]
    Option         "DPMS"
EndSection

```So that's where you can specify your monitor's capabilities. Make sure that the numbers match what your monitor can do.

If you still experience issues, you can add the bold line below to the device section.
```

Section "Device"
    Identifier     "NVIDIA Corporation NV44 [GeForce 6200 TurboCache]"
    Driver         "nvidia"
**    Option         "UseEdidFreqs" "False"**
EndSection

```


PS restart X with *<alt><printscreen>k*

---

### Post by Giblet5 on 2009-10-28
> **Wim Sturkenboom said:**
> So that's where you can specify your monitor's capabilities. Make sure that the numbers match what your monitor can do.

If you still experience issues, you can add the bold line below to the device section.

+1 on that.

Your monitor manufacturer will have those timings listed as something like "Horizontal Sync Frequency: 32-90Khz" or "Hsync 32-90Khz"...

Use the numbers they provide. Floating point is OK too, if they provide it.

It has to match the manufacturer's numbers for your EXACT monitor. [COLOR="DarkRed"]**Some monitors can be damaged by using the wrong values...**[/COLOR]

---

### Post by hoppipolla on 2009-10-28

### Post by Wim Sturkenboom on 2009-10-28
Done on the fly when you boot (and I guess that it's kept in memory). At least that's what is supposed to happen, but yeah, it does not always work properly.

You can follow what has happened in the xorg log (*/var/log/Xorg.0.log*)

---

You can follow what has happened in the xorg log (*/var/log/Xorg.0.log*)

Oh so, let's say I keep it as "Configured Monitor", but then afterwards put my own bits and bobs (like HorizSync) before "EndSection"... is that ok? :)

---

### Post by Wim Sturkenboom on 2009-10-29
Yep, that should do the trick.
